title: Re-pointing a brick wall
subtitle: Rake out failing mortar joints and re-point an external or internal brick wall
excerpt: How to rake out old mortar and re-point brick joints with fresh mortar. Covers reading the condition of existing mortar, mixing the right mortar specification, and the common joint profiles.
sourceNotes: BRE Good Building Guide 46. Collins Complete DIY Manual. Traditional UK bricklaying practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mortar specifically mixed for pointing, typically weaker than the structural mortar behind it so it sacrifices itself before the brick face does. The mix ratio varies with wall exposure.

A mortar joint finished with the mortar raked back 3 to 5 mm from the face of the brick. Creates a shadow line and a traditional appearance. Not suitable for highly exposed elevations where water can collect in the recess.
A mortar joint angled so water sheds away from the face of the brick. The most weatherproof joint profile for exposed elevations.

content.text: Failing mortar joints let water in behind the brick face, which begins a cycle of freeze-thaw damage that deteriorates the brick itself over a few decades. Re-pointing is maintenance, not cosmetics. The mortar mix matters: a re-point that is too hard locks moisture into the wall and causes spalling of the brick faces.
Step 1, Assess the joints

Run a key along the joints. Crumbling mortar comes away easily, these joints need re-pointing. Hard mortar that cannot be scratched but shows surface crazing is debatable; leave it unless water is visibly penetrating. Mortar that has shrunk away from the brick faces, leaving a gap on either side, is letting in water regardless of how hard it feels. The critical areas are horizontal bed joints on an exposed elevation (where water can pond) and damaged joints near windows and doors.
Step 2, Rake out the old mortar

Rake the joints to a minimum depth of 15 to 20 mm (two finger-widths) to give the new mortar enough bed to key into. A cold chisel and club hammer works for small areas; an angle grinder with a joint raking disc covers larger areas faster but risks damaging brick faces. An SDS drill with a chisel attachment works on soft mortar. Do not rake so hard that the brick arris (edge) is damaged, the brick face is more expensive to repair than the joint. Brush out all loose dust and fragments with a stiff brush.
Step 3, Mix the mortar
Match the new
pointing mortar

to the softness of the existing wall. For most Victorian and Edwardian brickwork: 1 part cement, 1 part hydraulic lime, 6 parts sharp sand (a 1:1:6 mix). This is softer than modern cement mortar and will not cause spalling. For modern hard-brick new build: 1 part cement, 4 parts builder's sand. Never use neat cement on older brickwork. Add water slowly; the mix is right when it holds its shape but does not crumble when squeezed.
Step 4, Dampen and point
Dampen the raked joints and surrounding brickwork with a wet brush, dry brickwork sucks the moisture out of fresh mortar too fast and causes shrinkage cracks. Load the
hawk
and work mortar into the joints with the filling knife, starting with the vertical perpend joints and then the horizontal bed joints. Press the mortar firmly into the back of the joint.
Step 5, Profile the joints
Once the mortar has stiffened slightly (roughly 30 minutes after applying, depending on temperature), shape the joint profile. A
weatherstruck joint

on an exposed elevation: angle the filling knife slightly and draw it along the joint so the mortar face tilts back at the top, shedding water forward. A flush joint on a sheltered or internal wall: draw the knife flat across the face, then brush lightly with a dry brush to expose the aggregate. Avoid ironing the joints smooth, a polished face cracks when it shrinks.
Variations
Internal brick walls: a softer mix of 1 part hydraulic lime to 2 parts sharp sand is appropriate for internal exposed brickwork. No cement is required for unexposed indoor conditions.

Listed buildings: consult the conservation officer before re-pointing. Hot lime mortars (lime putty, not hydraulic lime) are often required on listed structures. Standard bagged hydraulic lime is not appropriate for all historic contexts.
Care

Protect new pointing from frost for the first 48 hours, cover the area with hessian or a breathable frost blanket in cold weather. Do not let hoses or washing water run down a freshly pointed wall for 5 days.
Troubleshooter

items.symptom: New mortar cracks or shrinks away from brick edges
items.cause: Mortar too wet when applied, or raked joints too shallow for a good key
items.fix: Rake back the new mortar, re-rake the joints to 20 mm minimum depth, dampen, and re-point with a stiffer mix.

items.cause: Mortar mix too hard (high cement), trapping moisture in the brick which then freezes
items.fix: Rake out the hard mortar and re-point with a softer lime-based mix. Ongoing spalling may need a specialist assessment.
